metadata.working_title: A regression-based approach to phylogenetic reconstruction from multi-sample bulk DNA sequencing of tumors

abstract_sketch.problem: Multi-sample bulk DNA sequencing of tumors enables reconstruction of the clonal evolutionary history of a patient's cancer, but requires joint deconvolution of clones and inference of ancestral relationships from noisy variant allele frequencies.
abstract_sketch.gap: Existing methods for phylogenetic reconstruction from bulk sequencing data do not scale to modern datasets, which can contain dozens of samples and dozens of distinct sub-populations.
abstract_sketch.approach: We separate the reconstruction problem into a structured l1-regression sub-problem for a fixed clonal tree and an optimization over tree space. By exploiting the combinatorial structure of clonal matrices we derive an O(mnd) regression algorithm with warm-start capability under SPR moves, and use it to build fastBE, a deterministic search method for the l1-VAFFP.
abstract_sketch.key_results: On simulated data, fastBE matches or outperforms Pairtree, Orchard, CALDER, and CITUP in reconstruction accuracy while running up to two orders of magnitude faster; on 14 B-ALL patients and two patient-derived colorectal xenograft models, fastBE produces trees with fewer sum-condition violations than Pairtree.
abstract_sketch.impact: fastBE scales phylogenetic reconstruction from bulk tumor sequencing to thousands of clones and supports per-mutation phylogenies, expanding the scope of clonal analysis in cancer genomics.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Tumor evolution through somatic alterations and the role of multi-sample bulk sequencing
TracerX-style multi-region sequencing reveals hidden heterogeneity: up to 65% of subclones and 76% of subclonal mutations would be missed without it
Recent datasets reach up to 90 samples and 26 subclones per patient; existing methods fail to scale beyond ~10 subclones
Contribution: structured l1-regression formulation of VAF factorization, O(mnd) regression algorithm with warm starts, fastBE method, empirical gains on simulated, B-ALL, and PDX colorectal data
Roadmap of the paper

sections.title: Background and Related Work
content_bullets: Infinite sites model, clonal trees, n-clonal matrices, and the VAFFP
Frequency-matrix factorization F = UB and loss variants (L0, L1, L2, 0/1, probabilistic)
Prior methods: AncesTree, CITUP, LICHeE, CALDER, PhyloWGS, PASTRI, Pairtree, Orchard
Role of the sum condition as a necessary and sufficient condition for exact factorization
Analogy to minimum-evolution distance-based phylogenetics and FastME/FastTree

sections.title: A structured regression model for the l1-VAFFP

subsections.title: The l1-VAF regression sub-problem
content_bullets: Problem statement: given F and B, minimize |F - UB|_1 over u >= 0 with row sums <= 1
Reduce to Dot Product Tree Labeling Problem via LP duality and change of variables lambda_i = beta_i - alpha_i
Inverse identity B = (I - A)^{-1} and the appearance of the sum condition in the dual

subsections.title: An O(nd) algorithm for the DPTLP
content_bullets: Recurrence for g_i(psi) using dynamic programming on the tree
Class L_k of concave piecewise-linear functions with integer breakpoints; compact tuple representation (y, s_1, ..., s_{k+1})
Closure of L_k under addition and under the max-in-window operation yielding h_j in L_{k+1}
Theorem: DPTLP solved in O(nd) time

subsections.title: Warm-start updates under SPR operations
content_bullets: After pruning and regrafting subtree T_i at vertex j only vertices on paths to delta(i) or j change
SPR query in O(m d max{d(i), d(j)}) time; vertex-append in O(m d d(i))
Corollary 1 summarizes preprocessing O(mnd) time/space

subsections.title: fastBE: deterministic beam-style search over tree space
content_bullets: Iteratively append mutations in an order O = {o_1, ..., o_n}
For mutation o_i, find parent p and child-subset S to adopt that minimize the regression loss
Uses efficient recomputation from Theorem 1 instead of naive evaluation

subsections.title: Runtime comparison to linear programming solvers
content_bullets: 264 simulated (F, B) pairs; structured algorithm versus Gurobi v9.0.3 and CPLEX v22.1.0
Mean speedups of 95.6x over Gurobi and 105.1x over CPLEX (model-solve time only)
Warm-start under SPR: 6.2x mean speedup over cold start across 25,000 SPR-perturbed trees per instance

subsections.title: Evaluation of fastBE on simulated data
content_bullets: Small regime n in {3,5,10}, m in {5,10,25}: 108 simulated instances; termination counts fastBE 108/108, Pairtree 108/108, Orchard 108/108, CALDER 106/108, CITUP 107/108
n=10: mean F1 fastBE 0.965, Pairtree 0.972, Orchard 0.965
Modest regime n in {20,30,50}: CITUP and CALDER fail to terminate and are excluded; n=50 mean F1 fastBE 0.825, Pairtree 0.749, Orchard 0.805
fastBE mean 1.21 s at n=50; order-of-magnitude speedup over Pairtree and Orchard

Large regime n in {100,250,500,1000}, m in {50,100}: fastBE 1229.8 s mean at n=1000, 24/24 terminations; Orchard 71749.1 s mean, 19/24 terminations in 48h on 32 cores; mean F1 fastBE 0.773 vs Orchard 0.783
Sample efficiency: near-perfect recovery (median F1 0.987) at m >= 50, n < 100; sharp transition in accuracy near m/n = 1

subsections.title: B-progenitor acute lymphoblastic leukemia patients
content_bullets: 14 B-ALL patients, >200x targeted sequencing; per-patient samples median 42 (min 13, max 90)
fastBE applied on the mutation clusters from Pairtree; median 8 mutation clusters (min 3, max 17)
Inferred trees have median >= 8 distinct edges vs Pairtree
Normalized frequency-matrix estimation error < 1% except in one case; fastBE modestly lower
Mean total sum-condition violation V: fastBE 1.44 vs Pairtree 2.40 across 14 patients

subsections.title: Patient-derived colorectal cancer models
content_bullets: POP66 (8 samples, 25 clones) and CSC28 (4 samples, 11 clones) from Pal et al.
Total sum-condition violation CSC28: fastBE 0.273, Pairtree 0.473; POP66: fastBE 3.280, Pairtree 4.770
Per-sample trend holds, most pronounced for CPT-11 Resistant POP66 samples
Shannon diversity: similar for CSC28; fastBE finds higher heterogeneity in parent tumor and CPT-11 Resistant POP66 samples

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Restate: structured l1-regression view gives O(mnd) algorithm and fastBE, scaling VAFFP to ~1000 clones
Place fastBE alongside Pairtree, Orchard, CITUP, CALDER; note new ability to operate at mutation rather than cluster level
Biological implication: lower sum-condition violation on B-ALL and colorectal PDX data yields different heterogeneity interpretations
Limitations: tightness of O(mnd), restriction to SNVs in copy-neutral regions, no automatic clustering or uncertainty estimates
Future work: cancer cell fraction / descendant cell fraction, Dollo model, longitudinal consistency
